```sql
MySQL > select datediff(CAST('2007-12-31 23:59:59' AS DATETIME), CAST('2007-12-30' AS DATETIME));+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+| datediff(CAST('2007-12-31 23:59:59' AS DATETIME), CAST('2007-12-30' AS DATETIME)) |+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                                                 1 |+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+MySQL > select datediff(CAST('2010-11-30 23:59:59' AS DATETIME), CAST('2010-12-31' AS DATETIME));+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+| datediff(CAST('2010-11-30 23:59:59' AS DATETIME), CAST('2010-12-31' AS DATETIME)) |+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                                               -31 |+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```


#### References

[date_diff](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_diff/)


---

### day

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/day/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/day/)

day
Extracts the day part of a date or datetime expression and returns a value that ranges from 1 to 31.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT DAY(DATETIME|DATE date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select day('1987-01-31');+----------------------------+| day('1987-01-31 00:00:00') |+----------------------------+|                         31 |+----------------------------+MySQL > select day('1987-01-31 20:10:59');+----------------------------+| day('1987-01-31 20:10:59') |+----------------------------+|                         31 |+----------------------------+
```


#### keywords

DAY, day


---

### dayname

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayname/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayname/)

dayname
Returns the day corresponding to a date.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
VARCHAR DAYNAME(date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select dayname('2007-02-03 00:00:00');+--------------------------------+| dayname('2007-02-03 00:00:00') |+--------------------------------+| Saturday                       |+--------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

DAYNAME


---

### dayofmonth

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayofmonth/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayofmonth/)

dayofmonth
Obtains the day part in a date and returns a value that ranges from 1 to 31.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT DAYOFMONTH(DATETIME date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select dayofmonth('1987-01-31');+-----------------------------------+| dayofmonth('1987-01-31 00:00:00') |+-----------------------------------+|                                31 |+-----------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

DAYOFMONTH


---

### dayofweek

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayofweek/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayofweek/)

dayofweek
Returns the weekday index for a given date. For example, the index for Sunday is 1, for Monday is 2, for Saturday is 7.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type, or a valid expression that can be cast into a DATE or DATETIME value.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT dayofweek(DATETIME date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select dayofweek('2019-06-25');+----------------------------------+| dayofweek('2019-06-25 00:00:00') |+----------------------------------+|                                3 |+----------------------------------+MySQL > select dayofweek(cast(20190625 as date));+-----------------------------------+| dayofweek(CAST(20190625 AS DATE)) |+-----------------------------------+|                                 3 |+-----------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

DAYOFWEEK


---

### dayofweek_iso

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/day_of_week_iso/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/day_of_week_iso/)

dayofweek_iso
Returns the ISO standard day of the week for the specified date as an integer within the range of `1` to `7`. In this standard, `1` represents Monday, and `7` represents Sunday.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT DAY_OF_WEEK_ISO(DATETIME date)
```


#### Parameters

`date`: the date you want to convert. It must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Examples

The following example returns the ISO standard day of the week for the date `2023-01-01`:


```sql
MySQL > select dayofweek_iso('2023-01-01');+-----------------------------+| dayofweek_iso('2023-01-01') |+-----------------------------+|                           7 |+-----------------------------+
```


#### Keywords

DAY_OF_WEEK_ISO


---

### dayofyear

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayofyear/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/dayofyear/)

dayofyear
Returns the day of the year for a given date.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT DAYOFYEAR(DATETIME|DATE date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select dayofyear('2007-02-03 00:00:00');+----------------------------------+| dayofyear('2007-02-03 00:00:00') |+----------------------------------+|                               34 |+----------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

DAYOFYEAR


---

### days_add

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/days_add/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/days_add/)

days_add
Adds a specified number of days to a given date or date time.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME days_add(DATETIME|DATE date, INT n);
```


#### Parameters

`date`: a DATE or DATETIME expression.

`n`: the number of days to add.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value.

If any input parameter is NULL or invalid, NULL is returned.

If the output date exceeds the range of [0000-01-01 00:00:00, 9999-12-31 00:00:00], NULL is returned.


#### Examples


```sql
select days_add('2023-10-31 23:59:59', 1);+------------------------------------+| days_add('2023-10-31 23:59:59', 1) |+------------------------------------+| 2023-11-01 23:59:59                |+------------------------------------+select days_add('2023-10-31 23:59:59', 1000);+---------------------------------------+| days_add('2023-10-31 23:59:59', 1000) |+---------------------------------------+| 2026-07-27 23:59:59                   |+---------------------------------------+select days_add('2023-10-31', 1);+---------------------------+| days_add('2023-10-31', 1) |+---------------------------+| 2023-11-01 00:00:00       |+---------------------------+
```


#### Keywords

DAY,day


---

### days_diff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/days_diff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/days_diff/)

days_diff
Returns the day difference between two date expressions (`expr1` â `expr2`), accurate to the day.

Difference between days_diff and [datediff](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/datediff/):


| Function | Behavior | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| days_diff | The calculation is accurate to the second and rounded down to the nearest integer. | days_diff between '2020-12-25 23:00:00' and '2020-12-24 23:00:01' is 0. |
| datediff | The calculation is accurate to the day. | datediff between '2020-12-25 23:00:00' and '2020-12-24 23:00:01' is 1. |


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT days_diff(DATETIME expr1,DATETIME expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the end time. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.
- expr2: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.


#### Return value

Returns a BIGINT value.

NULL is returned if the date does not exist, for example, 2022-02-29. days_diff() takes the hour, minute, and second parts into calculation. If the difference is less than 1 day, 0 is returned.


#### Examples


```sql
select days_diff('2010-11-30 23:00:00', '2010-11-29 23:00:00')+---------------------------------------------------------+| days_diff('2010-11-30 23:00:00', '2010-11-29 23:00:00') |+---------------------------------------------------------+| 1                                                       |+---------------------------------------------------------+select days_diff('2010-11-30 23:00:00', '2010-11-29 23:10:00')+---------------------------------------------------------+| days_diff('2010-11-30 23:00:00', '2010-11-29 23:10:00') |+---------------------------------------------------------+| 0                                                       |+---------------------------------------------------------+
```


---

### days_sub

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/days_sub/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/days_sub/)

days_sub
Subtracts specified days from a date or datetime to get a new datetime.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME days_sub(DATETIME|DATE d, INT n);
```


#### Parameters

`d`: the date or datetime expression.

`n`: the number of days to subtract from the `d`.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the datetime type, NULL is returned if any parameter is NULL or invalid.

NULL is returned if the result exceeds the range [0000-01-01 00:00:00, 9999-12-31 00:00:00].


#### Examples


```sql
SELECT DAYS_SUB('2022-12-20 12:00:00', 10);+--------------------------------------------------+| days_sub('2022-12-20 12:00:00', INTERVAL 10 DAY) |+--------------------------------------------------+| 2022-12-10 12:00:00                              |+--------------------------------------------------+SELECT DAYS_SUB('2022-12-20 12:00:00', -10);+---------------------------------------------------+| days_sub('2022-12-20 12:00:00', INTERVAL -10 DAY) |+---------------------------------------------------+| 2022-12-30 12:00:00                               |+---------------------------------------------------+SELECT DAYS_SUB('2022-12-20 12:00:00', 738874);+------------------------------------------------------+| days_sub('2022-12-20 12:00:00', INTERVAL 738874 DAY) |+------------------------------------------------------+| 0000-01-01 12:00:00                                  |+------------------------------------------------------+SELECT DAYS_SUB('2022-12-20 12:00:00', 738875);+------------------------------------------------------+| days_sub('2022-12-20 12:00:00', INTERVAL 738875 DAY) |+------------------------------------------------------+| NULL                                                 |+------------------------------------------------------+SELECT DAYS_SUB('2022-12-20 12:00:00', -2913550);+--------------------------------------------------------+| days_sub('2022-12-20 12:00:00', INTERVAL -2913550 DAY) |+--------------------------------------------------------+| 9999-12-31 12:00:00                                    |+--------------------------------------------------------+SELECT DAYS_SUB('2022-12-20 12:00:00', -2913551);+--------------------------------------------------------+| days_sub('2022-12-20 12:00:00', INTERVAL -2913551 DAY) |+--------------------------------------------------------+| NULL                                                   |+--------------------------------------------------------+
```


#### keywords

DAY,day


---

### from_days

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/from_days/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/from_days/)

from_days
Returns a date from 0000-01-01.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE FROM_DAYS(INT N)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select from_days(730669);+-------------------+| from_days(730669) |+-------------------+| 2000-07-03        |+-------------------+
```


#### keyword

FROM_DAYS,FROM,DAYS


---

### from_unixtime

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/from_unixtime/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/from_unixtime/)

from_unixtime
Converts a UNIX timestamp into the required time format. The default format is `yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss`. It also supports the formats in [date_format](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_format/).

Currently, `string_format` supports the following formats:


```sql
%Y: Year  e.g.: 2014, 1900%m: Month   e.g.: 12, 09%d: Day  e.g.: 11, 01%H: Hour  e.g.: 23, 01, 12%i: Minute  e.g.: 05, 11%s: Second  e.g.: 59, 01
```

Other formats are invalid and NULL will be returned.


#### Syntax


```sql
VARCHAR from_unixtime(BIGINT unix_timestamp[, VARCHAR string_format])
```


#### Parameters

- unix_timestamp: the UNIX timestamp you want to convert. It must be of the BIGINT type. If the specified timestamp is less than 0 or larger than 253402243199, NULL will be returned. That is, the range for timestamp is1970-01-01 00:00:00to9999-12-30 11:59:59(varies because of timezone).
- string_format: the required time format.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME or DATE value of the VARCHAR type. If `string_format` specifies the DATE format, a DATE value of the VARCHAR type is returned.

If the timestamp exceeds the value range or if `string_format` is invalid, NULL will be returned.


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select from_unixtime(1196440219);+---------------------------+| from_unixtime(1196440219) |+---------------------------+| 2007-12-01 00:30:19       |+---------------------------+MySQL > select from_unixtime(1196440219, 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss');+--------------------------------------------------+| from_unixtime(1196440219, 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') |+--------------------------------------------------+| 2007-12-01 00:30:19                              |+--------------------------------------------------+MySQL > select from_unixtime(1196440219, '%Y-%m-%d');+-----------------------------------------+| from_unixtime(1196440219, '%Y-%m-%d')   |+-----------------------------------------+| 2007-12-01                              |+-----------------------------------------+MySQL > select from_unixtime(1196440219, '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s');+--------------------------------------------------+| from_unixtime(1196440219, '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s')   |+--------------------------------------------------+| 2007-12-01 00:30:19                              |+--------------------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

FROM_UNIXTIME,FROM,UNIXTIME


---

### hour

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hour/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hour/)

hour
Returns the hour for a given date. The return value ranges from 0 to 23.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT HOUR(DATETIME|DATE date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select hour('2018-12-31 23:59:59');+-----------------------------+| hour('2018-12-31 23:59:59') |+-----------------------------+|                          23 |+-----------------------------+
```


#### keyword

HOUR


---

### hours_add

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hours_add/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hours_add/)

hours_add
Adds hours to a date or datetime.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME hours_add(DATETIME|DATE date, INT hours);
```


#### Parameters

`date`: Base date or datetime.

`hours`: Hours to be added.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value.

NULL is returned when any of the input parameters is NULL.


#### Examples


```sql
select hours_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 2);+-------------------------------------+| hours_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 2) |+-------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 03:01:01                 |+-------------------------------------+select hours_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1);+--------------------------------------+| hours_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1) |+--------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 00:01:01                  |+--------------------------------------+select hours_add('2022-01-01', 1);+----------------------------+| hours_add('2022-01-01', 1) |+----------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:00:00        |+----------------------------+
```


---

### hours_diff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hours_diff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hours_diff/)

hours_diff
Returns the hour difference between two date expressions (`expr1` â `expr2`), accurate to the hour.


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT hours_diff(DATETIME expr1, DATETIME expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the end time. It must be of the DATETIME type.
- expr2: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME type.


#### Return value

Returns a BIGINT value.

NULL is returned if the date does not exist, for example, 2022-02-29.


#### Examples


```sql
select hours_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-11-30 20:58:59');+----------------------------------------------------------+| hours_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-11-30 20:58:59') |+----------------------------------------------------------+|                                                        3 |+----------------------------------------------------------+
```


---

### hours_sub

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hours_sub/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/hours_sub/)

hours_sub
Reduces the specified date and time by a specified number of hours.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME hours_sub(DATETIME|DATE date, INT hours);
```


#### Parameters

- date: It must be a valid DATE or DATETIME expression.
- hours: The number of hours reduced. The supported data type is INT.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value. If the date does not exist, for example, `2022-21-01`, or if the date is not a DATE or DATETIME value, NULL is returned.


#### Examples


```sql
select hours_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 1);+-------------------------------------+| hours_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 1) |+-------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 00:01:01                 |+-------------------------------------+select hours_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1);+--------------------------------------+| hours_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1) |+--------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 02:01:01                  |+--------------------------------------+select hours_sub('2022-01-01', 1);+----------------------------+| hours_sub('2022-01-01', 1) |+----------------------------+| 2021-12-31 23:00:00        |+----------------------------+select hours_sub('2022-01-01', -1);+-----------------------------+| hours_sub('2022-01-01', -1) |+-----------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:00:00         |+-----------------------------+Error case:select hours_sub('2022-21-01', -1);+--------------------------------------+| hours_sub('2022-21-01', -1) |+--------------------------------------+| NULL                                 |+--------------------------------------+
```


---

### jodatime_format

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/jodatime_format/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/jodatime_format/)

jodatime_format
Converts the specified date into a string in the specified Joda DateTimeFormat pattern format.


#### Syntax


```sql
VARCHAR JODATIME_FORMAT(DATETIME | DATE date, VARCHAR format)
```


#### Parameters

- date: the date you want to convert. It must be a valid date or date expression.
- format: the format of the date and time to be returned. For information about the available formats, seeJoda-Time format.


#### Return value

Currently, the return value is a string that contains a maximum of 128 bytes in length. If the length of the string converted from the specified date exceeds 128 bytes, `NULL` is returned.


#### Examples

Example 1: Convert the date `2020-06-25 15:58:51` into a string in `yyyy-MM-dd` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'yyyy-MM-dd');+------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'yyyy-MM-dd') |+------------------------------------------------------+| 2020-06-25                                           |+------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 2: Convert the date `2020-06-25 15:58:51` into a string in `yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss');+---------------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') |+---------------------------------------------------------------+| 2020-06-25 15:58:51                                           |+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 3: Convert the date `2020-06-25 15:58:51` into a string in `MM dd ee EE` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'MM dd ee EE');+-------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'MM dd ee EE') |+-------------------------------------------------------+| 06 25 04 Thu                                          |+-------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 4: Convert the date `2020-06-25 15:58:51` into a string in `MMM dd ee EEE` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'MMM dd ee EEE');+---------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'MMM dd ee EEE') |+---------------------------------------------------------+| Jun 25 04 Thu                                           |+---------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 5: Convert the date `2020-06-25 15:58:51` into a string in `MMMM dd ee EEEE` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'MMMM dd ee EEEE');+-----------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2020-06-25 15:58:51', 'MMMM dd ee EEEE') |+-----------------------------------------------------------+| June 25 04 Thursday                                       |+-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 6: Convert the date `2023-06-25 12:00:00` into a string in `KK:mm:ss a` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2023-06-25 12:00:00', 'KK:mm:ss a');+------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2023-06-25 12:00:00', 'KK:mm:ss a') |+------------------------------------------------------+| 00:00:00 PM                                          |+------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 7: Convert the date `2023-06-25 12:00:00` into a string in `hh:mm:ss a` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2023-06-25 12:00:00', 'hh:mm:ss a');+------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2023-06-25 12:00:00', 'hh:mm:ss a') |+------------------------------------------------------+| 12:00:00 PM                                          |+------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 8: Convert the date `2023-06-25 00:00:00` into a string in `yyyyMMdd ''starrocks''` format.


```sql
MySQL > select jodatime_format('2023-06-25 00:00:00', 'yyyyMMdd ''starrocks''');+------------------------------------------------------------------+| jodatime_format('2023-06-25 00:00:00', 'yyyyMMdd \'starrocks\'') |+------------------------------------------------------------------+| 20230625 starrocks                                               |+------------------------------------------------------------------+
```


#### Keywords

JODATIME_FORMAT, JODA, FORMAT


---

### last_day

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/last_day/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/last_day/)

last_day
Returns the last day of an input DATE or DATETIME expression based on the specified date part. For example, `last_day('2023-05-10', 'month')` returns the last day of the month in which '2023-05-10' falls.

If the date part is not specified, this function returns the last day of the month for a given date.

This function is supported from v3.1.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE last_day(DATETIME|DATE date_expr[, VARCHAR unit])
```


#### Parameters

- date_expr: a DATE or DATETIME expression, required.
- unit: the date part, optional. Valid values includemonth,quarter, andyear, default tomonth. Ifunitis invalid, an error is returned.


#### Return value

Returns a DATE value.


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select last_day('2023-05-10', 'month');+----------------------------------+| last_day('2023-05-10', 'month')  |+----------------------------------+| 2023-05-31                       |+----------------------------------+MySQL > select last_day('2023-05-10');+------------------------+| last_day('2023-05-10') |+------------------------+| 2023-05-31             |+------------------------+MySQL > select last_day('2023-05-10', 'quarter');+-----------------------------------+| last_day('2023-05-10', 'quarter') |+-----------------------------------+| 2023-06-30                        |+-----------------------------------+MySQL > select last_day('2023-05-10', 'year');+---------------------------------------+| last_day('2023-05-10', 'year')        |+---------------------------------------+| 2023-12-31                            |+---------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

LAST_DAY, LAST


---

### makedate

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/makedate/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/makedate/)

makedate
Creates and returns a date based on the given year and day of year values.

This function is supported from v3.1.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE makedate(INT year, INT dayOfYear);
```


#### Parameters

- year: It ranges from 0 to 9999. NULL is returned if this range is exceeded. The supported data type is INT.
- dayOfYear: the day of year. The supported data type is INT. In order to maintain the same semantics as the functiondayofyear, if this number is greater than 366 or is 366 in a non-leap year, it is not a day of year.


#### Return value

Returns the date on the dayOfYear-th day of the given year.

- yearmust be in the range of [0,9999]. Otherwise, NULL is returned.
- dayOfYearmust be between 1 and the number of days in the current year (365 in normal years, 366 in leap years). Otherwise, NULL is returned.
- The result is also NULL if either input parameter is NULL.


#### Examples


```sql
mysql> select makedate(2023,0);+-------------------+| makedate(2023, 0) |+-------------------+| NULL              |+-------------------+mysql> select makedate(2023,32);+--------------------+| makedate(2023, 32) |+--------------------+| 2023-02-01         |+--------------------+mysql> select makedate(2023,365);+---------------------+| makedate(2023, 365) |+---------------------+| 2023-12-31          |+---------------------+mysql> select makedate(2023,366);+---------------------+| makedate(2023, 366) |+---------------------+| NULL                |+---------------------+mysql> select makedate(9999,365);+---------------------+| makedate(9999, 365) |+---------------------+| 9999-12-31          |+---------------------+mysql> select makedate(10000,1);+--------------------+| makedate(10000, 1) |+--------------------+| NULL               |+--------------------+
```


#### keyword

MAKEDATE,MAKE


---

### microseconds_add

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/microseconds_add/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/microseconds_add/)

microseconds_add
Adds a time interval to a date value. The time interval is in microseconds.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME microseconds_add(DATETIME expr1,INT expr2);
```


#### Parameters

`expr1`: the time expression. It must be of the DATETIME type.

`expr2`: the time interval you want to add, in microseconds. It must be of the INT type.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the DATETIME type. If the input value is of the DATE type, the hour, minute, and seconds parts are processed as `00:00:00`.


#### Examples


```sql
select microseconds_add('2010-11-30 23:50:50', 2);+--------------------------------------------+| microseconds_add('2010-11-30 23:50:50', 2) |+--------------------------------------------+| 2010-11-30 23:50:50.000002                 |+--------------------------------------------+1 row in set (0.00 sec)select microseconds_add('2010-11-30', 2);+-----------------------------------+| microseconds_add('2010-11-30', 2) |+-----------------------------------+| 2010-11-30 00:00:00.000002        |+-----------------------------------+
```


---

### microseconds_sub

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/microseconds_sub/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/microseconds_sub/)

microseconds_sub
Subtracts a time interval from a date value. The time interval is in microseconds.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME microseconds_sub(DATETIME expr1,INT expr2);
```


#### Parameters

`expr1`: the time expression. It must be of the DATETIME type.

`expr2`: the time interval you want to substract, in microseconds. It must be of the INT type.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the DATETIME type. If the input value is of the DATE type, the hour, minute, and seconds parts are processed as `00:00:00`.


#### Examples


```sql
select microseconds_sub('2010-11-30 23:50:50', 2);+--------------------------------------------+| microseconds_sub('2010-11-30 23:50:50', 2) |+--------------------------------------------+| 2010-11-30 23:50:49.999998                 |+--------------------------------------------+select microseconds_sub('2010-11-30', 2);+-----------------------------------+| microseconds_sub('2010-11-30', 2) |+-----------------------------------+| 2010-11-29 23:59:59.999998        |+-----------------------------------+
```


---

### milliseconds_diff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/milliseconds_diff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/milliseconds_diff/)

milliseconds_diff
Returns the time difference between the start date and end date in milliseconds.

This function is supported from v3.2.4.


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT milliseconds_diff(DATETIME expr1, DATETIME expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the end time. It must be of the DATETIME type. Ifexpr1is earlier thanexpr2, a negative value is returned.
- expr2: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME type.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the BIGINT type. NULL is returned if the input date does not exist.


#### Examples


```sql
select milliseconds_diff('2024-01-25 21:00:00.423','2024-01-25 21:00:00.123');+-------------------------------------------------------------------------+| milliseconds_diff('2024-01-25 21:00:00.423', '2024-01-25 21:00:00.123') |+-------------------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                                     300 |+-------------------------------------------------------------------------+select milliseconds_diff('2024-01-25 21:00:01', '2024-01-25 21:00:00');+-----------------------------------------------------------------+| milliseconds_diff('2024-01-25 21:00:01', '2024-01-25 21:00:00') |+-----------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                            1000 |+-----------------------------------------------------------------+select milliseconds_diff('2024-01-25 00:00:01', '2024-01-25');+--------------------------------------------------------+| milliseconds_diff('2024-01-25 00:00:01', '2024-01-25') |+--------------------------------------------------------+|                                                   1000 |+--------------------------------------------------------+
```


---

### minute

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minute/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minute/)

minute
Returns the minute for a given date. The return value ranges from 0 to 59.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT MINUTE(DATETIME|DATE date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select minute('2018-12-31 23:59:59');+-----------------------------+|minute('2018-12-31 23:59:59')|+-----------------------------+|                          59 |+-----------------------------+
```


#### keyword

MINUTE


---

### minutes_add

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minutes_add/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minutes_add/)

minutes_add
Adds the specified minutes to the date, accurate to the minute.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME minutes_add(DATETIME|DATE date, INT minutes);
```


#### Parameters

- date: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.
- minutes: the minutes to add. It must be of the INT type, it could be greater, equal or less than zero.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value.

Returns NULL if either `date` or `minutes` is NULL.


#### Examples


```sql
select minutes_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 2);+---------------------------------------+| minutes_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 2) |+---------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:03:01                   |+---------------------------------------+select minutes_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1);+----------------------------------------+| minutes_add('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1) |+----------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:00:01                    |+----------------------------------------+select minutes_add('2022-01-01', 1);+------------------------------+| minutes_add('2022-01-01', 1) |+------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 00:01:00          |+------------------------------+
```


---

### minutes_diff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minutes_diff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minutes_diff/)

minutes_diff
Returns the minute difference between two date expressions (`expr1` â `expr2`), accurate to the minute.


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT minutes_diff(DATETIME expr1,DATETIME expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the end time. It must be of the DATETIME type.
- expr2: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME type.


#### Return value

Returns a BIGINT value.

NULL is returned if the date does not exist, for example, 2022-02-29.


#### Examples


```sql
select minutes_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-11-30 20:58:59');+------------------------------------------------------------+| minutes_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-11-30 20:58:59') |+------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                        181 |+------------------------------------------------------------+
```


---

### minutes_sub

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minutes_sub/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/minutes_sub/)

minutes_sub
Subtracts specified minutes from the date, accurate to the minute.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME minutes_sub(DATETIME|DATE date, INT minutes);
```


#### Parameters

- date: the base date. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.
- minutes: the minutes to reduce. It must be of the INT type, it could be greater, equal or less than zero.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value.

Returns NULL if either `date` or `minutes` is NULL.


#### Examples


```sql
select minutes_sub('2022-01-01 01:03:01', 2);+---------------------------------------+| minutes_sub('2022-01-01 01:03:01', 2) |+---------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:01:01                   |+---------------------------------------+select minutes_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1);+----------------------------------------+| minutes_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1) |+----------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:02:01                    |+----------------------------------------+select minutes_sub('2022-01-01', 1);+------------------------------+| minutes_sub('2022-01-01', 1) |+------------------------------+| 2021-12-31 23:59:00          |+------------------------------+
```


---

### month

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/month/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/month/)

month
Returns the month for a given date. The return value ranges from 1 to 12.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT MONTH(DATETIME date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select month('1987-01-01');+-----------------------------+|month('1987-01-01 00:00:00') |+-----------------------------+|                           1 |+-----------------------------+
```


#### keyword

MONTH


---

### monthname

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/monthname/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/monthname/)

monthname
Returns the name of the month for a given date.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
VARCHAR MONTHNAME(date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select monthname('2008-02-03 00:00:00');+----------------------------------+| monthname('2008-02-03 00:00:00') |+----------------------------------+| February                         |+----------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

MONTHNAME, monthname


---

### months_add

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/months_add/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/months_add/)

months_add
Adds a specified number of months to the date, accurate to the month.

The [add_months](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/add_months/) function provides similar functionalities.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME months_add(DATETIME expr1, INT expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.
- expr2: the months to add. It must be of the INT type. It can be greater, equal, or less than zero. A negative value subtracts months fromdate.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value.


#### Examples


```sql
select months_add('2019-08-01 13:21:03', 8);+--------------------------------------+| months_add('2019-08-01 13:21:03', 8) |+--------------------------------------+| 2020-04-01 13:21:03                  |+--------------------------------------+select months_add('2019-08-01', 8);+-----------------------------+| months_add('2019-08-01', 8) |+-----------------------------+| 2020-04-01 00:00:00         |+-----------------------------+select months_add('2019-08-01 13:21:03', -8);+---------------------------------------+| months_add('2019-08-01 13:21:03', -8) |+---------------------------------------+| 2018-12-01 13:21:03                   |+---------------------------------------+select months_add('2019-02-28 13:21:03', 1);+--------------------------------------+| months_add('2019-02-28 13:21:03', 1) |+--------------------------------------+| 2019-03-28 13:21:03                  |+--------------------------------------+select months_add('2019-01-30 13:21:03', 1);+--------------------------------------+| months_add('2019-01-30 13:21:03', 1) |+--------------------------------------+| 2019-02-28 13:21:03                  |+--------------------------------------+
```


---

### months_diff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/months_diff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/months_diff/)

months_diff
Returns the month difference between two date expressions (`expr1` â `expr2`), accurate to the month.


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT months_diff(DATETIME expr1,DATETIME expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the end time. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.
- expr2: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.


#### Return value

Returns a BIGINT value.

NULL is returned if the date does not exist, for example, 2022-02-29.


#### Examples


```sql
select months_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-1-1 23:59:59');+---------------------------------------------------------+| months_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-1-1 23:59:59') |+---------------------------------------------------------+|                                                      10 |+---------------------------------------------------------+select months_diff('2010-11-30', '2010-1-1');+---------------------------------------+| months_diff('2010-11-30', '2010-1-1') |+---------------------------------------+|                                    10 |+---------------------------------------+
```


---

### months_sub

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/months_sub/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/months_sub/)

months_sub
Subtracts specified months from the date.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME months_sub(DATETIME expr1, INT expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the specified date. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.
- expr2: the month to subtract. It must be INT type.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value.


#### Examples


```sql
select months_sub('2022-02-28 15:04:05', 1);+--------------------------------------+| months_sub('2022-02-28 15:04:05', 1) |+--------------------------------------+| 2022-01-28 15:04:05                  |+--------------------------------------+select months_sub('2022-02-28', 1);+-----------------------------+| months_sub('2022-02-28', 1) |+-----------------------------+| 2022-01-28 00:00:00         |+-----------------------------+
```


---

### next_day

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/next_day/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/next_day/)

next_day
Returns the date of the first specified day of week (DOW) that occurs after the input date (DATE or DATETIME). For example, `next_day('2023-04-06', 'Monday')` returns the date of the next Monday that occurred after '2023-04-06'.

This function is supported from v3.1. It is the opposite of [previous_day](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/previous_day/).


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE next_day(DATETIME|DATE date_expr, VARCHAR dow)
```


#### Parameters

- date_expr: the input date. It must be a valid DATE or DATETIME expression.
- dow: the day of week. Valid values include a number of abbreviations which are case-sensitive:DOW_FULLDOW_2DOW_3SundaySuSunMondayMoMonTuesdayTuTueWednesdayWeWedThursdayThThuFridayFrFriSaturdaySaSat


#### Return value

Returns a DATE value.

Any invalid `dow` will cause an error. `dow` is case-sensitive.

Returns NULL if an invalid date or a NULL argument is passed in.


#### Examples


```sql
-- Return the date of the next Monday that occurred after 2023-04-06. 2023-04-06 is Thursday and the date of the next Monday is 2023-04-10.MySQL > select next_day('2023-04-06', 'Monday');+----------------------------------+| next_day('2023-04-06', 'Monday') |+----------------------------------+| 2023-04-10                       |+----------------------------------+MySQL > select next_day('2023-04-06', 'Tue');+-------------------------------+| next_day('2023-04-06', 'Tue') |+-------------------------------+| 2023-04-11                    |+-------------------------------+MySQL > select next_day('2023-04-06 20:13:14', 'Fr');+---------------------------------------+| next_day('2023-04-06 20:13:14', 'Fr') |+---------------------------------------+| 2023-04-07                            |+---------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

NEXT_DAY, NEXT


---

### now, current_timestamp, localtime, localtimestamp

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/now/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/now/)

now, current_timestamp, localtime, localtimestamp
Returns the current date and time.

From 3.1.6 onwards, this function can accept a precision input (`p`), which represents the number of digits to retain after seconds. A maximum of 6 digits can be retained (accurate to the microsecond). The digits after `p` are padded with 0. If `p` is not specified, a DATETIME value accurate to the second is returned.

The following example returns date and time values when different precision is specified.


```sql
mysql > select now(),now(1),now(2),now(3),now(4),now(5),now(6)\G*************************** 1. row *************************** now(): 2023-12-08 13:46:45now(1): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.100000now(2): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.110000now(3): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115000now(4): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115800now(5): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115840now(6): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115843
```

This function may return different results for different time zones. For more information, see [Configure a time zone](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/administration/management/timezone/).


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME NOW()DATETIME NOW(INT p)
```


#### Parameters

`p`: optional, the specified precision, that is, the number of digits to retain after seconds. It must be an INT value within the range of [1,6]. `select now(0)` is equivalent to `select now()`.


#### Return value

- Ifpis not specified, this function returns a DATETIME value accurate to the second.
- Ifpis specified, this function returns a date and time value of the specified precision.


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select now();+---------------------+| now()               |+---------------------+| 2019-05-27 15:58:25 |+---------------------+MySQL > select now(),now(1),now(2),now(3),now(4),now(5),now(6)\G*************************** 1. row *************************** now(): 2023-12-08 13:46:45now(1): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.100000now(2): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.110000now(3): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115000now(4): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115800now(5): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115840now(6): 2023-12-08 13:46:45.115843
```


#### keyword

NOW, now


---

### previous_day

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/previous_day/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/previous_day/)

previous_day
Returns the date of the first specified day of week (DOW) that occurs before the input date (DATE  or DATETIME). For example, `previous_day('2023-04-06', 'Monday')` returns the date of the previous Monday that occurred before '2023-04-06'.

This function is supported from v3.1. It is the opposite of [next_day](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/next_day/).


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE previous_day(DATETIME|DATE date_expr, VARCHAR dow)
```


#### Parameters

- date_expr: the input date. It must be a valid DATE or DATETIME expression.
- dow: the day of week. Valid values include a number of abbreviations which are case-sensitive:DOW_FULLDOW_2DOW_3SundaySuSunMondayMoMonTuesdayTuTueWednesdayWeWedThursdayThThuFridayFrFriSaturdaySaSat


#### Return value

Returns a DATE value.

Any invalid `dow` will cause an error. `dow` is case-sensitive.

NULL is returned if an invalid date or a NULL argument is passed in.


#### Examples


```sql
-- Return the date of the previous Monday that occurred before 2023-04-06. 2023-04-06 is Thursday and the date of the previous Monday is 2023-04-03.MySQL > select previous_day('2023-04-06', 'Monday');+--------------------------------------+| previous_day('2023-04-06', 'Monday') |+--------------------------------------+| 2023-04-03                           |+--------------------------------------+MySQL > select previous_day('2023-04-06', 'Tue');+-----------------------------------+| previous_day('2023-04-06', 'Tue') |+-----------------------------------+| 2023-04-04                        |+-----------------------------------+MySQL > select previous_day('2023-04-06 20:13:14', 'Fr');+-------------------------------------------+| previous_day('2023-04-06 20:13:14', 'Fr') |+-------------------------------------------+| 2023-03-31                                |+-------------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

PREVIOUS_DAY, PREVIOUS, previousday


---

### quarter

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/quarter/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/quarter/)

quarter
Returns the quarter part of a date, in the range of 1 to 4.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT quarter(DATETIME|DATE date);
```


#### Parameters

`date`: It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.


#### Return value

Returns an INT value.

NULL is returned in any of the following scenarios:

- The date is not a valid DATETIME or DATE value.
- The input is empty.
- The date does not exist, for example, 2022-02-29.


#### Examples

Example 1: Return the quarter part of a DATETIME value.


```sql
SELECT QUARTER("2022-10-09 15:59:33");+--------------------------------+| quarter('2022-10-09 15:59:33') |+--------------------------------+|                              4 |+--------------------------------+
```

Example 2: Return the quarter part of a DATE value.


```sql
SELECT QUARTER("2022-10-09");+-----------------------+| quarter('2022-10-09') |+-----------------------+|                     4 |+-----------------------+
```

Example 3: Return the quarter part that corresponds to the current time or date.


```sql
SELECT QUARTER(NOW());+----------------+| quarter(now()) |+----------------+|              4 |+----------------+SELECT QUARTER(CURDATE());+--------------------+| quarter(curdate()) |+--------------------+|                  4 |+--------------------+
```


---

### sec_to_time

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/sec_to_time/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/sec_to_time/)

sec_to_time

#### Description

The SEC_TO_TIME function converts a value in seconds into a TIME type, returning the result in the format HH:MM :SS .
The input seconds represent the time elapsed since the start of a day (00:00:00).


#### Syntax


```sql
TIME sec_to_time(BIGINT sec)
```


#### Parameters

`sec`: It must be of the INT type.


#### Return value

Returns a TIME value in the format HH:MM :SS , representing the time calculated from the start of a day (00:00:00).
If sec is NULL, the function returns NULL.


#### Examples


```sql
select sec_to_time(43994);+-----------------------------+| sec_to_time(43994)          |+-----------------------------+|                   '12:13:14'|+-----------------------------+
```


---

### second

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/second/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/second/)

second
Returns the second part for a given date. The return value ranges from 0 to 59.

The `date` parameter must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
INT SECOND(DATETIME date)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select second('2018-12-31 23:59:59');+-----------------------------+|second('2018-12-31 23:59:59')|+-----------------------------+|                          59 |+-----------------------------+
```


#### keyword

SECOND


---

### seconds_add

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/seconds_add/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/seconds_add/)

seconds_add

#### description


##### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME SECONDS_ADD(DATETIME expr1,INT expr2)
```

Add a specified time interval to the date. Unit is second.

The expr1 parameter is a valid datetime expression.

The expr2 parameter is the seconds you want to add.


#### example


```sql
select seconds_add('2010-11-30 23:50:50', 2);+---------------------------------------+| seconds_add('2010-11-30 23:50:50', 2) |+---------------------------------------+| 2010-11-30 23:50:52                   |+---------------------------------------+select seconds_add('2010-11-30', 2);+------------------------------+| seconds_add('2010-11-30', 2) |+------------------------------+| 2010-11-30 00:00:02          |+------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

SECONDS_ADD,ADD


---

### seconds_diff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/seconds_diff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/seconds_diff/)

seconds_diff
Returns the second difference between two date expressions (`expr1` â `expr2`), accurate to the second.


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT seconds_diff(DATETIME expr1,DATETIME expr2);
```


#### Parameters

- expr1: the end time. It must be of the DATETIME type.
- expr2: the start time. It must be of the DATETIME type.


#### Return value

Returns a BIGINT value.

NULL is returned if the date does not exist, for example, 2022-02-29.


#### Examples


```sql
select seconds_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-11-30 20:59:59');+------------------------------------------------------------+| seconds_diff('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '2010-11-30 20:59:59') |+------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                      10800 |+------------------------------------------------------------+
```


---

### seconds_sub

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/seconds_sub/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/seconds_sub/)

seconds_sub
Subtracts a time interval from a date value. The time interval is in seconds.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME seconds_sub(DATETIME|DATE date, INT seconds);
```


#### Parameters

`date`: the time expression. It must be of the DATETIME type.

`seconds`: the time interval you want to substract, in seconds. It must be of the INT type.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the DATETIME type.

If the input value is of the DATE type, the hour, minute, and seconds parts are processed as `00:00:00`.


#### Examples


```sql
select seconds_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 2);+---------------------------------------+| seconds_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', 2) |+---------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:00:59                   |+---------------------------------------+select seconds_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1);+----------------------------------------+| seconds_sub('2022-01-01 01:01:01', -1) |+----------------------------------------+| 2022-01-01 01:01:02                    |+----------------------------------------+select seconds_sub('2022-01-01', 1);+------------------------------+| seconds_sub('2022-01-01', 1) |+------------------------------+| 2021-12-31 23:59:59          |+------------------------------+v
```


---

### str2date

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str2date/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str2date/)

str2date
Converts a string into a DATE value according to the specified format. If the conversion fails, NULL is returned.

The format must be consistent with that described in [date_format](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_format/).

This function is equivalent to [str_to_date](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str_to_date/) but has a different return type.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE str2date(VARCHAR str, VARCHAR format);
```


#### Parameters

`str`: the time expression you want to convert. It must be of the VARCHAR type.

`format`: the format used to return the value. For the supported format, see [date_format](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_format/).


#### Return value

Returns a value of the DATE type.

NULL is returned if `str` or `format` is NULL.


#### Examples


```sql
select str2date('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s');+------------------------------------------------------+| str2date('2010-11-30 23:59:59', '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s') |+------------------------------------------------------+| 2010-11-30                                           |+------------------------------------------------------+1 row in set (0.01 sec)
```


---

### str_to_date

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str_to_date/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str_to_date/)

str_to_date
Converts a string into a DATETIME value according to the specified format. If the conversion fails, NULL is returned.

The format must be consistent with that described in [date_format](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_format/).

This function is inverse to [date_format](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_format/).


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME STR_TO_DATE(VARCHAR str, VARCHAR format)
```


#### Parameters

`str`: the time expression you want to convert. It must be of the VARCHAR type.

`format`: the format used to return the value. For the supported format, see [date_format](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/date_format/).


#### Return value

Returns a value of the DATETIME type. If `format` specifies a date, a DATE value is returned.

NULL is returned if `str` or `format` is NULL.


#### Examples

Example 1: Convert the input into a DATETIME value.


```sql
MySQL > select str_to_date('2014-12-21 12:34:56', '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s');+---------------------------------------------------------+| str_to_date('2014-12-21 12:34:56', '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s') |+---------------------------------------------------------+| 2014-12-21 12:34:56                                     |+---------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 2: Convert the input into a DATE value.


```sql
MySQL > select str_to_date('2014-12-21 12:34:56', '%Y-%m-%d');+--------------------------------------------------------------+| str_to_date('2014-12-21 12:34:56', '%Y-%m-%d')               |+--------------------------------------------------------------+| 2014-12-21                                                   |+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 3: Convert the input "200442 Monday" into a DATE value.


```sql
MySQL > select str_to_date('200442 Monday', '%X%V %W');+-----------------------------------------+| str_to_date('200442 Monday', '%X%V %W') |+-----------------------------------------+| 2004-10-18                              |+-----------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

STR_TO_DATE,STR,TO,DATE


---

### str_to_jodatime

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str_to_jodatime/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/str_to_jodatime/)

str_to_jodatime
Converts a Joda-formatted string into a DATETIME value in the specified Joda DateTime format like `yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss`.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME str_to_jodatime(VARCHAR str, VARCHAR format)
```


#### Parameters

- str: the time expression you want to convert. It must be of the VARCHAR type.
- format: the Joda DateTime format of the DATETIME value to be returned. For information about the formats available, seeJoda DateTime.


#### Return value

- If parsing the input string succeeds, a DATETIME value is returned.
- If parsing the input string fails,NULLis returned.


#### Examples

Example 1: Convert the string `2014-12-21 12:34:56` into a DATETIME value in `yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss` format.


```sql
MySQL > select str_to_jodatime('2014-12-21 12:34:56', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss');+--------------------------------------------------------------+| str_to_jodatime('2014-12-21 12:34:56', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') |+--------------------------------------------------------------+| 2014-12-21 12:34:56                                          |+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 2: Convert the string `21/December/23 12:34:56` with a text-style month into a DATETIME value in `dd/MMMM/yy HH:mm:ss` format.


```sql
MySQL > select str_to_jodatime('21/December/23 12:34:56', 'dd/MMMM/yy HH:mm:ss');+------------------------------------------------------------------+| str_to_jodatime('21/December/23 12:34:56', 'dd/MMMM/yy HH:mm:ss') |+------------------------------------------------------------------+| 2023-12-21 12:34:56                                              |+------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 3: Convert the string `21/December/23 12:34:56.123` accurate to milliseconds into a DATETIME value in `dd/MMMM/yy HH:mm:ss.SSS` format.


```sql
MySQL > select str_to_jodatime('21/December/23 12:34:56.123', 'dd/MMMM/yy HH:mm:ss.SSS');+--------------------------------------------------------------------------+| str_to_jodatime('21/December/23 12:34:56.123', 'dd/MMMM/yy HH:mm:ss.SSS') |+--------------------------------------------------------------------------+| 2023-12-21 12:34:56.123000                                               |+--------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```


#### Keywords

STR_TO_JODATIME, DATETIME


---

### time_format

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/time_format/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/time_format/)

time_format
Formats TIME-type values in the specified format.


#### Syntax


```sql
VARCHAR TIME_FORMAT(TIME time, VARCHAR format)
```


#### Parameters

- time(Required): The time value (of the TIME type) to be formatted.
- format(Required): The format to use. Valid values:


```sql
%f	Microseconds (000000 to 999999)%H	Hour (00 to 23)%h	Hour (00 to 12)%i	Minutes (00 to 59)%p	AM or PM%S	Seconds (00 to 59)%s	Seconds (00 to 59)
```


#### Examples


```sql
mysql> SELECT TIME_FORMAT("19:30:10", "%h %i %s %p");+----------------------------------------+| time_format('19:30:10', '%h %i %s %p') |+----------------------------------------+| 12 00 00 AM                            |+----------------------------------------+1 row in set (0.01 sec)
```


---

### time_slice

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/time_slice/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/time_slice/)

time_slice
Converts a given time into the beginning or end of a time interval based on the specified time granularity.

This function is supported from v2.3.

v2.5 supports converting a given time into the end of a time interval.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME time_slice(DATETIME dt, INTERVAL N type[, boundary])
```


#### Parameters

- dt: the time to convert, DATETIME.
- INTERVAL N type: the time granularity, for example,interval 5 second.Nis the length of time interval. It must be an INT value.typeis the unit, which can be YEAR, QUARTER, MONTH, WEEK, DAY, HOUR, MINUTE, SECOND, MILLISECOND (since 3.1.7), and MICROSECOND (since 3.1.7).
- boundary: optional. It is used to specify whether to return the beginning (FLOOR) or end (CEIL) of the time interval. Valid values: FLOOR, CEIL. If this parameter is not specified,FLOORis the default. This parameter is supported from v2.5.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the DATETIME type.


#### Usage notes

The time interval starts from A.D. `0001-01-01 00:00:00`.


#### Examples

The following examples are provided based on the `test_all_type_select` table.


```sql
select * from test_all_type_select order by id_int;+------------+---------------------+--------+| id_date    | id_datetime         | id_int |+------------+---------------------+--------+| 2052-12-26 | 1691-12-23 04:01:09 |      0 || 2168-08-05 | 2169-12-18 15:44:31 |      1 || 1737-02-06 | 1840-11-23 13:09:50 |      2 || 2245-10-01 | 1751-03-21 00:19:04 |      3 || 1889-10-27 | 1861-09-12 13:28:18 |      4 |+------------+---------------------+--------+5 rows in set (0.06 sec)
```

Example 1: Convert a given DATETIME value to the beginning of a 5-second time interval without specifying the `boundary` parameter.


```sql
select time_slice(id_datetime, interval 5 second)from test_all_type_selectorder by id_int;+---------------------------------------------------+| time_slice(id_datetime, INTERVAL 5 second, floor) |+---------------------------------------------------+| 1691-12-23 04:01:05                               || 2169-12-18 15:44:30                               || 1840-11-23 13:09:50                               || 1751-03-21 00:19:00                               || 1861-09-12 13:28:15                               |+---------------------------------------------------+5 rows in set (0.16 sec)
```

Example 2: Convert a given DATETIME value to the beginning of a 5-day time interval with  `boundary` set to `FLOOR`.


```sql
select time_slice(id_datetime, interval 5 day, FLOOR)from test_all_type_selectorder by id_int;+------------------------------------------------+| time_slice(id_datetime, INTERVAL 5 day, floor) |+------------------------------------------------+| 1691-12-22 00:00:00                            || 2169-12-16 00:00:00                            || 1840-11-21 00:00:00                            || 1751-03-18 00:00:00                            || 1861-09-12 00:00:00                            |+------------------------------------------------+5 rows in set (0.15 sec)
```

Example 3: Convert a given DATETIME value to the end of a 5-day time interval.


```sql
select time_slice(id_datetime, interval 5 day, CEIL)from test_all_type_selectorder by id_int;+-----------------------------------------------+| time_slice(id_datetime, INTERVAL 5 day, ceil) |+-----------------------------------------------+| 1691-12-27 00:00:00                           || 2169-12-21 00:00:00                           || 1840-11-26 00:00:00                           || 1751-03-23 00:00:00                           || 1861-09-17 00:00:00                           |+-----------------------------------------------+
```

Example 4: Convert a given DATETIME value to the end of a 1-millisecond time interval.


```sql
select id_datetime, time_slice(id_datetime, interval 1 millisecond, CEIL)from test_all_type_selectorder by id_int;+---------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+| id_datetime         | time_slice(id_datetime, INTERVAL 1 millisecond, ceil) |+---------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+| 1691-12-23 04:01:09 | 1691-12-23 04:01:09.001000                            || 2169-12-18 15:44:31 | 2169-12-18 15:44:31.001000                            || 1840-11-23 13:09:50 | 1840-11-23 13:09:50.001000                            || 1751-03-21 00:19:04 | 1751-03-21 00:19:04.001000                            || 1861-09-12 13:28:18 | 1861-09-12 13:28:18.001000                            |+---------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+
```

Example 5: Convert a given DATETIME value to the end of a 1-microsecond time interval.


```sql
select id_datetime, time_slice(id_datetime, interval 1 microsecond, CEIL)from test_all_type_selectorder by id_int;+---------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+| id_datetime         | time_slice(id_datetime, INTERVAL 1 microsecond, ceil) |+---------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+| 1691-12-23 04:01:09 | 1691-12-23 04:01:09.000001                            || 2169-12-18 15:44:31 | 2169-12-18 15:44:31.000001                            || 1840-11-23 13:09:50 | 1840-11-23 13:09:50.000001                            || 1751-03-21 00:19:04 | 1751-03-21 00:19:04.000001                            || 1861-09-12 13:28:18 | 1861-09-12 13:28:18.000001                            |+---------------------+-------------------------------------------------------+
```


---

### time_to_sec

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/time_to_sec/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/time_to_sec/)

time_to_sec
Converts a time value into the number of seconds. The formula used for the conversion is as follows:

Hour x 3600 + Minute x 60 + Second


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT time_to_sec(TIME time)
```


#### Parameters

`time`: It must be of the TIME type.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the BIGINT type. If the input is invalid, NULL is returned.


#### Examples


```sql
select time_to_sec('12:13:14');+-----------------------------+| time_to_sec('12:13:14')     |+-----------------------------+|                        43994|+-----------------------------+
```


---

### timediff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timediff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timediff/)

timediff
Returns the difference between two DATETIME expressions.

The return value must be of the TIME type.


#### Syntax


```sql
TIME TIMEDIFF(DATETIME expr1, DATETIME expr2)`
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > SELECT TIMEDIFF(now(),utc_timestamp());+----------------------------------+| timediff(now(), utc_timestamp()) |+----------------------------------+| 08:00:00                         |+----------------------------------+MySQL > SELECT TIMEDIFF('2019-07-11 16:59:30','2019-07-11 16:59:21');+--------------------------------------------------------+| timediff('2019-07-11 16:59:30', '2019-07-11 16:59:21') |+--------------------------------------------------------+| 00:00:09                                               |+--------------------------------------------------------+MySQL > SELECT TIMEDIFF('2019-01-01 00:00:00', NULL);+---------------------------------------+| timediff('2019-01-01 00:00:00', NULL) |+---------------------------------------+| NULL                                  |+---------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

TIMEDIFF


---

### timestamp

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timestamp/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timestamp/)

timestamp
Returns the DATETIME value of a date or datetime expression.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME timestamp(DATETIME|DATE expr);
```


#### Parameters

`expr`: the time expression you want to convert. It must be of the DATETIME or DATE type.


#### Return value

Returns a DATETIME value. If the input time is empty or does not exist, such as `2021-02-29`, NULL is returned.


#### Examples


```sql
select timestamp("2019-05-27");+-------------------------+| timestamp('2019-05-27') |+-------------------------+| 2019-05-27 00:00:00     |+-------------------------+1 row in set (0.00 sec)
```


---

### timestampadd

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timestampadd/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timestampadd/)

timestampadd
Adds an integer expression interval to the date or datetime expression `datetime_expr`.

The unit for the interval as mentioned must be one of the following:

MILLISECOND (since 3.2), SECOND, MINUTE, HOUR, DAY, WEEK, MONTH, or YEAR.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME TIMESTAMPADD(unit, interval, DATETIME datetime_expr)
```


#### Parameters

- datetime_expr: the DATE or DATETIME value to which you want to add a time interval.
- interval: an integer expression that specifies the number of intervals to add.
- unit: the unit of the time interval to add. Supported units include MILLISECOND (since 3.2), SECOND, MINUTE, HOUR, DAY, WEEK, MONTH, and YEAR.


#### Return value

Returns a value of the same data type as `datetime_expr`.


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > SELECT TIMESTAMPADD(MINUTE,1,'2019-01-02');+------------------------------------------------+| timestampadd(MINUTE, 1, '2019-01-02 00:00:00') |+------------------------------------------------+| 2019-01-02 00:01:00                            |+------------------------------------------------+MySQL > SELECT TIMESTAMPADD(WEEK,1,'2019-01-02');+----------------------------------------------+| timestampadd(WEEK, 1, '2019-01-02 00:00:00') |+----------------------------------------------+| 2019-01-09 00:00:00                          |+----------------------------------------------+MySQL > SELECT TIMESTAMPADD(MILLISECOND,1,'2019-01-02');+--------------------------------------------+| timestampadd(MILLISECOND, 1, '2019-01-02') |+--------------------------------------------+| 2019-01-02 00:00:00.001000                 |+--------------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

TIMESTAMPADD


---

### timestampdiff

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timestampdiff/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/timestampdiff/)

timestampdiff
Returns the interval from `datetime_expr2` to `datetime_expr1`. `datetime_expr1` and `datetime_expr2` must be of the DATE or DATETIME type.

The unit for the integer result and the interval should be one of the following:

MILLISECOND (since 3.2), SECOND, MINUTE, HOUR, DAY, WEEK, MONTH, or YEAR.


#### Syntax


```sql
BIGINT TIMESTAMPDIFF(unit,DATETIME datetime_expr1, DATETIME datetime_expr2)
```


#### Parameters

- datetime_expr: the two DATE or DATETIME expressions you want to compare.
- unit: the unit of the time difference. Supported units include MILLISECOND (since 3.2), SECOND, MINUTE, HOUR, DAY, WEEK, MONTH, and YEAR.


#### Return value

Returns a BIGINT value.


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL> SELECT TIMESTAMPDIFF(MONTH,'2003-02-01','2003-05-01');+--------------------------------------------------------------------+| timestampdiff(MONTH, '2003-02-01 00:00:00', '2003-05-01 00:00:00') |+--------------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                                  3 |+--------------------------------------------------------------------+MySQL> SELECT TIMESTAMPDIFF(YEAR,'2002-05-01','2001-01-01');+-------------------------------------------------------------------+| timestampdiff(YEAR, '2002-05-01 00:00:00', '2001-01-01 00:00:00') |+-------------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                                -1 |+-------------------------------------------------------------------+MySQL> SELECT TIMESTAMPDIFF(MINUTE,'2003-02-01','2003-05-01 12:05:55');+---------------------------------------------------------------------+| timestampdiff(MINUTE, '2003-02-01 00:00:00', '2003-05-01 12:05:55') |+---------------------------------------------------------------------+|                                                              128885 |+---------------------------------------------------------------------+MySQL > SELECT TIMESTAMPDIFF(MILLISECOND,'2003-02-01','2003-05-01');+--------------------------------------------------------+| timestampdiff(MILLISECOND, '2003-02-01', '2003-05-01') |+--------------------------------------------------------+|                                             7689600000 |+--------------------------------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

TIMESTAMPDIFF


---

### to_date

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_date/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_date/)

to_date
Converts a DATETIME value into a date.


#### Syntax


```sql
DATE TO_DATE(DATETIME datetime)
```


#### Examples


```sql
MySQL > select to_date("2020-02-02 00:00:00");+--------------------------------+| to_date('2020-02-02 00:00:00') |+--------------------------------+| 2020-02-02                     |+--------------------------------+
```


#### keyword

TO_DATE


---

### to_datetime

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_datetime/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_datetime/)

to_datetime
Converts a Unix timestamp to a DATETIME type value **based on the current time zone settings**.

For detailed instructions on setting a time zone, see [Configure a time zone](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/administration/management/timezone/).

If you want to convert a Unix timestamp to a DATETIME type value independent of the current session's time zone settings, you can use [to_datetime_ntz](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_datetime_ntz/).


#### Syntax


```sql
DATETIME to_datetime(BIGINT unix_ts, INT scale)
```


#### Parameters


| Name | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| unix_ts | BIGINT | Yes | The Unix timestamp to convert. For example,1598306400(seconds) and1598306400123(milliseconds). |
| scale | INT | No | Time precision. Valid values:0indicates seconds (Default).3indicates milliseconds.6indicates microseconds. |


#### Return Value

- On success: returns aDATETIMEvalue based on the current session's time zone.
- On failure: returnsNULL. Common reasons include:Invalidscale(not 0, 3, or 6)Value out of DATETIME range (0001-01-01 to 9999-12-31)


#### Example


```sql
SET time_zone = 'Asia/Shanghai';SELECT to_datetime(1598306400);-- Returns: 2020-08-25 06:00:00SELECT to_datetime(1598306400123, 3);-- Returns: 2020-08-25 06:00:00.123000
```


---

### to_datetime_ntz

**Source:** [https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_datetime_ntz/](https://docs.starrocks.io/docs/sql-reference/sql-functions/date-time-functions/to_datetime_ntz/)

to_datetime_ntz
Converts a Unix timestamp to a DATETIME type value. **This function is always based on UTC+0, regardless of the time zone settings.**
